fact: The MV Doña Paz was carrying over four thousand passengers despite being licensed for 1,493
source: Wikipedia, citing Philippine maritime investigation reports and news coverage

fact: The ferry had no passenger manifest — no complete record of who was on board
source: Widely documented; reported extensively by AP and Reuters at the time. See also: Sulpicio Lines investigation records referenced in maritime disaster literature

fact: The Doña Paz collided with an oil tanker (the MT Vector) and spilled fuel ignited, setting the surrounding sea on fire
source: Philippine Coast Guard and Maritime Industry Authority (MARINA) investigation findings; summarized in multiple sources

fact: There were grossly insufficient life jackets — roughly two for every hundred passengers
source: MARINA investigation report; referenced in 'The Doña Paz Disaster' coverage by the Philippine Daily Inquirer and international press. Exact ratio varies by source but severe shortage is widely documented.

fact: No confirmed final death toll was ever established, and many victims were never reported missing
source: Widely documented. Official estimates cite over 4,386 dead, making it the deadliest peacetime maritime disaster in history. The absence of a manifest made a precise count impossible.

fact: No lookout was posted on the Doña Paz at the time of the collision (December 20, 1987)
source: MARINA and Philippine Board of Marine Inquiry findings; survivor testimony indicated crew negligence
